content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 49 (GEN_049)

Genesis 49 is the most philologically difficult chapter in Genesis — archaic poetry, hapax legomena, and disputed meanings throughout. Under the expanded disagreement rules, every verse where the three translators produced meaningfully different English renderings is logged below.

---

## 1. Be'acharit Ha-Yamim — "In the Latter Days" vs. "In the Days to Come" (v. 1)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the latter days" | Preserves the eschatological weight of be'acharit |
| LOGOS | "in the latter days" | Same — follows KJV/NASB tradition |
| PNEUMA | "in the days to come" | Dynamic rendering; softens eschatological force |
| **SOPHIA** | **"in the latter days" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The phrase be'acharit ha-yamim is a fixed eschatological formula appearing in Num 24:14, Deut 31:29, Isa 2:2, Mic 4:1. "In the latter days" preserves this prophetic-oracular register. PNEUMA's "days to come" is merely temporal and loses the eschatological coloring. |

## 2. Reshit Oni — "Beginning of My Strength" vs. "Firstfruits of My Strength" vs. "First Fruit of My Vigor" (v. 3)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the beginning of my strength" | reshit = beginning; on = strength/vigor |
| LOGOS | "the firstfruits of my strength" | reshit as firstfruits — agricultural image |
| PNEUMA | "the first fruit of my vigor" | Same as LOGOS but "vigor" for on |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the firstfruits of my strength" — LOGOS upheld** | reshit carries the connotation of "first portion" or "firstfruits" (as in reshit qatsir, "firstfruits of harvest"), which fits the firstborn context. "Beginning" (EMET) is too abstract. "Vigor" (PNEUMA) is valid for on but "strength" echoes the parallel with koach ("my might") better. |

## 3. Preeminent vs. Supreme (v. 3)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "preeminent in dignity and preeminent in power" | yeter se'et ve-yeter az |
| LOGOS | "preeminent in dignity and preeminent in power" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "supreme in rank, supreme in power" | Dynamic rendering |
| **SOPHIA** | **"preeminent in dignity and preeminent in power" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | "Preeminent" preserves yeter (excess, preeminence). "Supreme" (PNEUMA) works but loses the specific Hebrew word's flavor. "Dignity" for se'et (from nasa, to lift up) is more precise than "rank." |

## 4. Pachaz Ka-Mayim — "Reckless as Water" vs. "Unstable as Water" vs. "Turbulent as Floodwater" (v. 4)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Reckless as water" | pachaz = moral recklessness (Arabic cognate) |
| LOGOS | "Unstable as water" | pachaz = instability (KJV tradition) |
| PNEUMA | "turbulent as floodwater" | Dynamic rendering — adds "flood" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Unstable as water" — LOGOS upheld** | The hapax pachaz encompasses both moral recklessness and physical instability. "Unstable" is the established English-Bible tradition (KJV, RSV, ESV) and captures the double sense — character instability and water's formlessness. "Reckless" (EMET) skews purely moral, losing the water image. "Turbulent as floodwater" (PNEUMA) adds "flood" not in the Hebrew and emphasizes violence over the character indictment. |

## 5. Kelei Chamas Mekherteihem — "Instruments of Violence" vs. "Weapons of Violence" (v. 5)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "instruments of violence are their swords" | kelei = instruments/vessels; literal |
| LOGOS | "weapons of violence are their swords" | kelei = weapons (contextual) |
| PNEUMA | "their swords are weapons of violence" | Reversed word order; "weapons" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"weapons of violence are their swords" — LOGOS upheld** | kelei in a military context means "weapons" (cf. 1 Sam 8:12, kelei milchamah). "Instruments" (EMET) is technically accurate but overly generic. Hebrew word order (predicate-first) is preserved per LOGOS. PNEUMA reverses to English order, losing the emphatic Hebrew structure. |

## 6. Nephesh and Kavod — "My Soul" / "My Glory" vs. "Me" / "Me" (v. 6)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Into their council let not my soul enter — in their assembly let not my glory be joined" | Literal: nephesh = soul, kavod = glory; Hebrew word order |
| LOGOS | "Let not my soul enter their council; let not my glory be joined to their assembly" | Same vocabulary, English word order |
| PNEUMA | "Let me never join their council, let me never be part of their assembly" | Paraphrases both nephesh and kavod as "me" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Let not my soul enter their council; let not my glory be joined to their assembly" — LOGOS upheld** | nephesh ("soul") and kavod ("glory") are specific, theologically loaded Hebrew terms that should not be collapsed into "me." The parallelism between soul and glory is deliberate — Jacob stakes his inner life (nephesh) and his reputation/honor (kavod) on distance from violence. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses both. LOGOS's English word order is preferable to EMET's inverted Hebrew order for readability. |

## 7. Evratam — "Fury" vs. "Wrath" vs. "Wrath" / "Cruel" vs. "Savage" (v. 7)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "their fury, for it is cruel" | evratam = fury; qashtah = cruel/hard |
| LOGOS | "their wrath, for it is cruel" | evratam = wrath |
| PNEUMA | "their wrath, for it is savage" | qashtah = savage |
| **SOPHIA** | **"their wrath, for it is cruel" — LOGOS upheld** | "Wrath" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the standard rendering for evrah and pairs well with "anger" in the preceding colon. "Fury" (EMET) is valid but less distinct from "anger." "Cruel" (EMET/LOGOS) is the precise rendering of qashtah (hard, harsh, cruel); "savage" (PNEUMA) intensifies beyond the Hebrew. |

## 8. "On the Neck of Your Enemies" vs. "At Your Enemies' Throats" (v. 8)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "your hand shall be on the neck of your enemies" | Literal: yadkha be-oref oyvekha |
| LOGOS | "your hand shall be on the neck of your enemies" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "your hand will be at your enemies' throats" | Dynamic: "necks" → "throats" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"your hand shall be on the neck of your enemies" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The Hebrew oref means specifically "back of the neck" or "nape" — the image is of enemies turning their backs and fleeing, with Judah's hand on their necks. "Throats" (PNEUMA) suggests frontal combat, which reverses the Hebrew image. |

## 9. Gur Aryeh — Word Order and Lion Terms (v. 9)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "A lion's cub is Judah" — "he crouched down" — "he lay down like a lion" — "who shall rouse him?" | Hebrew word order (gur aryeh Yehudah); kara = crouched |
| LOGOS | "Judah is a lion's cub" — "He stooped down; he crouched as a lion" — "who dares rouse him?" | English word order; ravats = crouched |
| PNEUMA | "Judah is a young lion" — "He crouches, he lies down like a lion" — "who dares rouse him?" | "young lion" for gur aryeh; present tense |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Judah is a lion's cub" — LOGOS upheld** | English word order is preferable for readability. "Lion's cub" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves gur aryeh more precisely than "young lion" (PNEUMA). "Who dares rouse him?" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) captures the rhetorical force better than "who shall rouse him?" (EMET). LOGOS's verb choices ("stooped down; he crouched") preserve the two distinct Hebrew verbs (kara, ravats). |

## 10. Ammim — "Peoples" vs. "Nations" (v. 10)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the obedience of the peoples" | ammim = peoples |
| LOGOS | "the obedience of the peoples" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the nations will yield their obedience" | ammim = nations; restructured as verb |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the obedience of the peoples" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | ammim means "peoples" (distinct from goyim, "nations"). The noun phrase yiqhat ammim ("obedience of peoples") should remain a noun phrase; PNEUMA restructures it as a verb clause, which changes the Hebrew grammar. |

## 11. Soreqah — "Choice Vine" vs. "Choicest Branch" (v. 11)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the choice vine" | soreqah = choice/select vine |
| LOGOS | "the choice vine" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the choicest branch" | Dynamic rendering — vine → branch |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the choice vine" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | soreqah is specifically a choice or select vine (from soreq, a high-quality grape variety). "Branch" (PNEUMA) loses the vine-specific vocabulary. |

## 12. Kibbes — "Washes" vs. "Has Washed" (v. 11)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he washes his garments in wine" | Present/prophetic tense |
| LOGOS | "he has washed his garments in wine" | Perfect tense |
| PNEUMA | "He washes his garments in wine" | Present tense |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he washes his garments in wine" — EMET/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | The prophetic/descriptive present captures the oracular register — Jacob is describing Judah's future prosperity as if witnessing it. The perfect (LOGOS) suggests a completed action that doesn't fit the prophetic context. |

## 13. Zebulun's Shore — "A Shore for Ships" vs. "A Harbor for Ships" / "Flank" vs. "Border" (v. 13)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he shall be a shore for ships — and his flank toward Sidon" | chof = shore; yerakhato = his flank/thigh |
| LOGOS | "he shall be a shore for ships, and his flank shall rest toward Sidon" | Same; adds "shall rest" |
| PNEUMA | "become a harbor for ships; his border will reach toward Sidon" | chof = harbor; yerakhato = border |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he shall be a shore for ships, and his flank shall extend toward Sidon" — LOGOS base, adjusted** | chof means "shore/coast" (not specifically "harbor"). "Flank" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the bodily metaphor of yerakhato (from yarekh, "thigh/side"), which is more vivid than "border" (PNEUMA). |

## 14. Rovets — "Crouching" vs. "Lying Down" (v. 14)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "crouching between the sheepfolds" | rovets = crouching/lying down |
| LOGOS | "crouching between the sheepfolds" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "lying down between the sheepfolds" | Dynamic rendering |
| **SOPHIA** | **"crouching between the sheepfolds" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | rovets denotes the specific posture of an animal settling down with legs folded under — "crouching" captures this better than generic "lying down." The same verb was used for the lion in v. 9 (ravats); the echo should be preserved. |

## 15. Mas Oved — "Forced-Laborer" vs. "Servant Doing Forced Labor" vs. "Laboring Slave" (v. 15)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a forced-laborer" | mas oved = corvée laborer |
| LOGOS | "a servant doing forced labor" | Expanded rendering |
| PNEUMA | "a laboring slave" | Dynamic rendering |
| **SOPHIA** | **"a servant at forced labor" — Blend** | mas oved is a technical term for corvée labor (cf. Josh 16:10, Judg 1:28). "Forced-laborer" (EMET) is efficient but hyphenated. "A servant doing forced labor" (LOGOS) is wordy. "Laboring slave" (PNEUMA) introduces "slave" (eved), which is a different Hebrew term. "A servant at forced labor" preserves both the servitude and the forced nature. |

## 16. Shephiphon — "Horned Viper" vs. "Viper" (v. 17)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a horned viper on the path" | shephiphon = horned viper specifically |
| LOGOS | "a viper by the path" | Generic viper |
| PNEUMA | "a viper along the path" | Generic viper |
| **SOPHIA** | **"a horned viper by the path" — EMET upheld** | shephiphon is a specific species — the horned viper (Cerastes cerastes), which lies half-buried in sand beside desert paths. "Horned" preserves the zoological specificity, as with "swarm" for dagah in 48:16 — distinctive vocabulary deserves distinctive English. |

## 17. Lishuat'kha Qivviti — Word Order (v. 18)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For your salvation I wait, O LORD" | Hebrew word order: object-verb-vocative |
| LOGOS | "I wait for your salvation, O LORD" | English word order |
| PNEUMA | "I wait for your salvation, O LORD" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"For your salvation I wait, O LORD" — EMET upheld** | The Hebrew fronts the object lishu'atekha ("for your salvation") for emphasis — the salvation is what Jacob's whole life has been oriented toward. English allows this fronting ("For your salvation I wait") without awkwardness, and it preserves the emphasis. This is the first appearance of yeshu'ah in Genesis; the emphatic position deserves retention. |

## 18. Lechem vs. Food — "Bread" vs. "Food" / "Dainties" vs. "Delicacies" (v. 20)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his bread shall be rich — and he shall yield royal dainties" | lechem = bread (literal) |
| LOGOS | "Asher's food shall be rich, and he shall yield royal delicacies" | lechem = food (idiomatic) |
| PNEUMA | "Asher's food will be rich, and he will yield royal delicacies" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Asher's food shall be rich, and he shall yield royal delicacies" — LOGOS upheld** | lechem in oracular/poetic context means "food" broadly (cf. its general sense in many passages). "Bread" (EMET) narrows unnecessarily. "Delicacies" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is more natural than "dainties" (EMET) in modern English. |

## 19. Imrei Shepher — "Beautiful Words" vs. "Beautiful Fawns" (v. 21) — MAJOR
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who gives beautiful words" | imrei = words (from omer); shepher = beauty |
| LOGOS | "he gives beautiful words" | Same reading |
| PNEUMA | "that bears beautiful fawns" | Reads imrei as related to "lambs/fawns" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he gives beautiful words" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The MT reads ha-noten imrei-shapher. The word imrei is unambiguously "words/sayings" (from omer), not "fawns." PNEUMA's reading follows an alternative interpretation that reads amarim as related to lambs, but this requires emendation. The MT vocalization supports "words." The image is of Naphtali as a graceful doe whose speech is beautiful — perhaps evoking the tribe's lyric tradition (Deborah's song, Judg 5). |

## 20. Ben Porat — "A Son of Fruitfulness" vs. "A Fruitful Bough" / "Daughters" vs. "Branches" (v. 22) — MAJOR
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "A son of fruitfulness is Joseph — a son of fruitfulness by a spring — daughters stride over a wall" | Literal: ben porat = son of fruitfulness; banot = daughters |
| LOGOS | "Joseph is a fruitful bough, a fruitful bough by a spring; his branches run over the wall" | porat = bough (plant image); banot = branches |
| PNEUMA | "Joseph is a fruitful bough, a fruitful bough by a spring — his branches climb over the wall" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Joseph is a fruitful bough, a fruitful bough by a spring; his branches run over the wall" — LOGOS upheld** | The phrase ben porat is an image of a fruit-bearing vine or tree. While EMET's "son of fruitfulness" is etymologically defensible, the botanical context (spring, wall) makes "fruitful bough" the natural reading. The word banot (literally "daughters") is used metaphorically for branches/tendrils of a vine (cf. the "daughters" of a tree climbing a wall). "Branches" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) captures the vine metaphor; "daughters stride over a wall" (EMET) is opaque in English. |

## 21. Ba'alei Chitsim — "Masters of Arrows" vs. "Harassed Him Severely" vs. "Pressed Him Hard" (v. 23)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the masters of arrows" | ba'alei chitsim = lords/masters of arrows |
| LOGOS | "shot at him, and harassed him severely" | Interprets the phrase as verbal action |
| PNEUMA | "they shot at him and pressed him hard" | Same interpretive approach |
| **SOPHIA** | **"they shot at him and harassed him — the masters of arrows" — Blend** | The Hebrew has three clauses: va-yemarruhu (they attacked him bitterly), va-robbu (and they shot), va-yistemhu ba'alei chitsim (the masters of arrows hated/harassed him). EMET preserves "masters of arrows" but drops the verb. LOGOS/PNEUMA drop "masters of arrows." The blend preserves both elements. |

## 22. Etan / Supple / Agile / Nimble (v. 24)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his bow remained firm — the arms of his hands were made supple" | eitan = firm; va-yafozu = were made supple/nimble |
| LOGOS | "his bow remained unmoved; his arms were made agile" | eitan = unmoved; "agile" |
| PNEUMA | "his bow remained steady, his arms were made nimble" | eitan = steady; "nimble" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"his bow remained steady, and the arms of his hands were made agile" — Blend** | eitan means "firm, enduring, steady" — "steady" (PNEUMA) captures the sense of unshaken constancy. "Arms of his hands" (EMET) preserves the Hebrew construct zro'ei yadav, which LOGOS/PNEUMA shorten. "Agile" (LOGOS) best renders va-yafozu (the gold/fine-metal connotation of paz suggests nimble, swift movement). |

## 23. "From There" vs. "By the Name of" vs. "By" (v. 24)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from there is the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel" | mi-sham = from there |
| LOGOS | "by the name of the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel" | Reads mi-shem (from the name) |
| PNEUMA | "by the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel" | Simplified |
| **SOPHIA** | **"from there, the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel" — EMET upheld** | The MT reads mi-sham ("from there"), not mi-shem ("from the name"). LOGOS's "by the name of" presupposes an emendation. The phrase "from there" points back to the divine source — from the hands of the Mighty One of Jacob comes the Shepherd and Stone of Israel. |

## 24. El Avikha — "The God of Your Father" vs. "Your Father's God" (v. 25)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from the God of your father who will help you" | me-El avikha = from the God of your father |
| LOGOS | "by the God of your father who will help you" | Same construct, different preposition |
| PNEUMA | "by your father's God, who helps you" | "your father's God" — reversed; present tense |
| **SOPHIA** | **"by the God of your father, who will help you" — LOGOS upheld** | "The God of your father" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew construct El avikha as a title-phrase. "Your father's God" (PNEUMA) is possessive English that loses the formulaic weight. "Will help" (future, EMET/LOGOS) fits the oracular/prophetic register better than "helps" (PNEUMA present). |

## 25. Birkhot Shadayim Va-Racham — "Breasts and of the Womb" vs. "Breast and Womb" (v. 25)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "blessings of the breasts and of the womb" | shadayim (dual) va-racham |
| LOGOS | "blessings of the breasts and of the womb" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "blessings of the breast and womb" | Drops "of the" — simplified |
| **SOPHIA** | **"blessings of the breasts and of the womb" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The Hebrew shadayim is dual ("breasts," not "breast"), and the repeated preposition "of the" preserves the construct chain's weight. The Shaddai/shadayim wordplay is critical (see EMET's note); preserving the full form honors it. |

## 26. Horai — "My Progenitors" vs. "My Ancestors" vs. "The Ancient Mountains" (v. 26) — MAJOR
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "over the blessings of my progenitors" | horai = my progenitors (from horeh, parent) |
| LOGOS | "beyond the blessings of my ancestors" | horai = my ancestors |
| PNEUMA | "surpass the blessings of the ancient mountains" | Reads horai as harai = mountains |
| **SOPHIA** | **"surpass the blessings of my progenitors" — EMET upheld (meaning), PNEUMA upheld (verb)** | The MT reads birkhot horay — "blessings of my progenitors/parents." PNEUMA reads horai as harai ("mountains"), following the LXX (oreon aidiōn, "eternal mountains") and Deut 33:15 parallel. Both readings are ancient. The MT vocalization supports "progenitors" — Jacob's blessings exceed those given to Abraham and Isaac. "Progenitors" (EMET) is more precise than "ancestors" (LOGOS). The verb: "surpass" (PNEUMA) is cleaner than "prevail over" (EMET) or "are mighty beyond" (LOGOS). |

## 27. Ta'avat vs. Bound vs. Bounty — Giv'ot Olam (v. 26)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to the bound of the everlasting hills" | ta'avat = bound/limit/desire |
| LOGOS | "up to the bounty of the everlasting hills" | ta'avat = bounty |
| PNEUMA | "the bounty of the everlasting hills" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to the utmost bound of the everlasting hills" — EMET upheld, expanded** | ta'avat (from avah) means "desire, longing, limit, boundary" — the blessings extend to the farthest limit of the everlasting hills. "Bounty" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) conflates ta'avat with a meaning it doesn't carry. "Bound" captures the spatial extent. |

## 28. Qodqod Nezir — "Crown / Consecrated One" vs. "Brow / One Set Apart" (v. 26)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "on the crown of the consecrated one of his brothers" | qodqod = crown of head; nezir = consecrated one |
| LOGOS | "upon the brow of the one set apart among his brothers" | qodqod = brow; nezir = set apart |
| PNEUMA | "on the brow of the one set apart among his brothers" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"on the crown of the head of the one set apart among his brothers" — Blend** | qodqod means "crown of the head" (the top/apex, cf. Deut 33:16, 2 Sam 14:25). "Brow" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the forehead, not the same body part. "Crown of the head" preserves qodqod precisely. For nezir: "set apart" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) captures the root nazar better for English readers than "consecrated" (EMET), since the separation motif is the point of the Joseph narrative — sold away, elevated, distinguished. |

## 29. Ze'ev Yitraf — "A Wolf That Tears" vs. "A Ravenous Wolf" (v. 27)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Benjamin is a wolf that tears" | ze'ev yitraf — literal: a wolf, it tears |
| LOGOS | "Benjamin is a ravenous wolf" | Interprets yitraf as "ravenous" |
| PNEUMA | "Benjamin is a ravenous wolf" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Benjamin is a wolf that tears" — EMET upheld** | The Hebrew ze'ev yitraf is two words: "wolf" + "he tears" (from taraf, to tear/rend). "A wolf that tears" preserves the verbal force. "Ravenous" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is an adjective that interprets rather than translates — it shifts from the act (tearing) to the character (ravenous). The tearing verb connects to the prey vocabulary in the next line and to taraf in Gen 37:33 (Joseph's coat: "he has surely been torn to pieces") — a dark echo. |

## 30. Shalal — "Plunder" vs. "Spoil" (v. 27)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he divides plunder" | shalal = plunder/spoil |
| LOGOS | "he divides the spoil" | Same meaning, "spoil" |
| PNEUMA | "he divides the plunder" | "plunder" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he divides the spoil" — LOGOS upheld** | "Spoil" is the traditional biblical English for shalal (cf. Isa 53:12, "he shall divide the spoil"). Both "plunder" and "spoil" are valid; "spoil" maintains the biblical register. |

## 31. Al-Penei Mamre — "Before Mamre" vs. "East of Mamre" vs. "Near Mamre" (v. 30)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "which is before Mamre" | al-penei = before/in front of |
| LOGOS | "to the east of Mamre" | Interprets "before" as eastward |
| PNEUMA | "near Mamre" | Generalized proximity |
| **SOPHIA** | **"which faces Mamre" — Blend** | al-penei literally means "upon the face of" — i.e., facing, opposite. "Before" (EMET) is ambiguous in English. "East of" (LOGOS) imports a compass direction not in the Hebrew. "Near" (PNEUMA) is too vague. "Which faces Mamre" captures the Hebrew directional idiom. This matches the rendering in Gen 23:17, 19 where the same phrase appears. |

## 32. "Gathered His Feet" vs. "Drew His Feet Up" (v. 33)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he gathered his feet into the bed" | va-ye'esof raglav — literal |
| LOGOS | "he drew his feet up into the bed" | Dynamic |
| PNEUMA | "he drew his feet up into the bed" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he gathered his feet into the bed" — EMET upheld** | The verb is asaph (to gather) — the same root used for "gathered to his people" in the next clause. This verbal echo is deliberate: Jacob gathers his feet, then is gathered to his people. The double asaph is one of the chapter's framing devices (see also v. 1: he'asephu, "gather yourselves"). "Drew up" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) loses this connection. |

## 33. "Commanding" vs. "Giving Instructions" (v. 33)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Jacob finished commanding his sons" | le-tsavvot = to command |
| LOGOS | "Jacob finished commanding his sons" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Jacob finished giving instructions to his sons" | Dynamic paraphrase |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Jacob finished commanding his sons" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | tsavah means "to command" — it carries patriarchal authority. "Giving instructions" (PNEUMA) softens the imperative force. |

---

## Minor Recurring Patterns (Not Individually Logged)

**Shall vs. Will:** EMET and LOGOS consistently use "shall" (prophetic/formal); PNEUMA uses "will" (modern/natural). SOPHIA follows "shall" for the oracular sections (vv. 3–27) to preserve prophetic register, and "will" for prose narrative (vv. 28–33).

**v. 12:** Full agreement — "His eyes are darker than wine, and his teeth whiter than milk."

**v. 29:** Minor — "I am being gathered" (EMET) vs. "I am to be gathered" (LOGOS) vs. "I am about to be gathered" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA: "I am to be gathered" (LOGOS).

**v. 31:** Minor word-order — "Sarah his wife" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "his wife Sarah" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows MT order: "Sarah his wife."

**v. 32:** Minor structural — "The purchase of the field" (EMET) vs. "the field... purchased" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). SOPHIA: "the field and the cave that is in it, purchased from the sons of Heth."

---

*Genesis 49: 33 disagreements logged across 31 verses (only v. 12 showed full 3-0 agreement). This is the highest disagreement density of any Genesis chapter, reflecting the extreme philological difficulty of Jacob's Blessing. SOPHIA ruled with EMET alone on 8 items (vv. 17, 18, 23-blend, 25, 27, 29, 32, 33), with LOGOS alone on 8 items (vv. 2-reshit, 5, 7, 8, 9, 11-choice-vine, 20, 24-God-of-father), with EMET/LOGOS together on 6 items (vv. 1, 3-preeminent, 6, 10, 14, 25-breasts), with LOGOS/PNEUMA on 1 item (v. 4), and produced blends on 10 items (vv. 13, 15, 21-blend, 22, 23, 24-eitan, 26-horai, 27-bound, 28-nezir, 30). The major interpretive divergences were: pachaz (reckless/unstable/turbulent), ben porat (son-of-fruitfulness/fruitful-bough), banot (daughters/branches), imrei shepher (words/fawns), horai (progenitors/mountains), ze'ev yitraf (wolf-that-tears/ravenous-wolf), and the v. 18 word order (Hebrew-front vs. English-natural). Full agreements (3-0): v. 12 (eyes darker than wine) and the Shiloh rendering (all three kept "Shiloh" as a proper noun, per instructions).*
